_comment: The COSMETIC CATALOG — every appearance piece the player could wear, defined as data (no code changes to add one). This is the 'inventory' half of player customization: which items exist, what SLOT each fills, and which WORLD granted it. The player's owned set + equipped loadout live separately in user://player_appearance.json (see PlayerAppearance); this file is the shared dictionary both the wardrobe and the renderer look items up in. Presentation-only: nothing here touches game logic. A 'sheet' that doesn't exist degrades silently (the layer is skipped), matching SpriteSlot's discipline, so new items can be declared before their art is drawn.
_slots_comment: SLOTS are the paper-doll layers, drawn back-to-front by ascending 'z'. Exactly one item occupies a slot at a time. 'required':true means the slot must always resolve to something — PlayerAppearance falls back to the slot's base default so the avatar can never render empty. Leave room between z values so new layers can slot between without renumbering (same spacing discipline as the companion arbiter bands).
_color_slots_comment: COLOR slots are the 'palette' half of paper-doll + palette: cheap personalization that recolors a layer without new art (skin tone, hair color). 'applies_to' names the paper-doll slot the recolor targets; 'ramps' are the named choices; 'default' is what a fresh player gets. The recolor itself (a palette-swap shader over the layer) is a DEFERRED presentation wiring — the data model and the resolved per-layer 'palette' field are in place now so the door is open; AvatarCompositor reads them once the shader lands.
_items_comment: ITEMS keyed by a WORLD-NAMESPACED id ('origin:name'), mirroring the companion's area ids ('world:region') — so a world's drops are self-contained and a later authoritative server can grant them by id without collision. 'origin':'base' items are owned by every player from the start and are the slot defaults (one per required slot via 'default_for'). The render fields (sheet/frame/fps/walk_frames/idle_frame/dirs/z) follow the SAME sheet convention as the existing player/companion art (rows=facing, cols=walk cycle); see SpriteActor. Non-base items below are DECLARED EXAMPLES of the world-grant pattern — their art isn't drawn yet, so they render as nothing until both the sheet exists and the player has obtained + equipped them.
